body_txt: When the villain has the hero captured, he will not shoot him on the spot . Instead, he treats the hero as a guest in his home and offers him a luxurious bedroom and a lovely meal. The hero might attempt to take a Cutlery Escape Aid during the meal, or dampen the festivities by simply saying " I'm Not Hungry ". This is generally in an effort to convince the hero to join them in a We Can Rule Together scenario, and will feature a sales pitch describing the villain's scheme . The hero may be provided with formal dining attire: a tux for the gent and a good-looking, well-fitting dress for the lady, with a bath if needed. This may explicitly be so the villain can ogle her. This differs from general Affably Evil behaviour, where the villain is likely to be genuinely friendly rather than faking it, though there's room for overlap. Treating the hero as a guest is a demonstration of unassailable power, confidence, and civility . It ratchets up the tension with the ever-present threat of unpleasantness should the hero step out of line. The hero will ultimately be placed into the Death Trap device upon their rejection. This will present the hero with an opportunity to escape, as per a Bond Villain Stupidity plot. Compare Captive Date , where one side of a romantic evening would rather not be there but isn't allowed to leave. The discount version of this trope is Kidnapper's KFC , when a kidnapper can only afford fast food for himself and his hostage because he's running from the cops.
